summary: The agent→parent→browser alert flow is coherent, typechecks cleanly, and the 34 targeted alert/metrics/history/agent tests pass. However, first-time notification enablement is effectively broken on browsers requiring a user gesture, and there are several smaller correctness and lifecycle issues. The full suite was not clean in this read-only environment: temporary-directory tests hit EROFS and one unchanged process-stream test failed.

findings.issue: Notification permission is requested automatically during component initialization, outside a user gesture. Browsers commonly suppress or reject such requests. Since `notify.show` is explicitly a no-op until permission is granted and there is no later user-triggered permission path, first-time users can receive no OS alerts at all.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit “Enable notifications” control and call `notify.requestPermission()` from its click handler. Surface denied/unsupported status so users understand why notifications are inactive.

findings.issue: The promises returned by `setAppBadge` and `clearAppBadge` are discarded without rejection handling. Feature presence does not guarantee success; security, permission, or document-state failures can therefore produce unhandled promise rejections whenever this effect runs.
findings.suggestion: Route both operations through a small async helper with `try/catch`, or attach `.catch(...)` while preserving the unsupported-API no-op.

findings.issue: The host-card alert count reads the raw cell without considering entry liveness. Cell subscriptions retain their last value across a dropped link, so a disconnected host continues showing an undimmed red “N alerts” indicator even though the badge correctly excludes that same stale value. This presents stale state as a live alert.
findings.suggestion: Gate the count on `entry.state().kind === "connected"`, or explicitly render a visually stale/last-known indicator when disconnected.

findings.issue: `alertsEqual` is not true set equality when either array contains duplicates. Equal array lengths plus one-way membership can consider `{items:["cpu","mem"]}` equal to `{items:["cpu","cpu"]}`. The current producer emits unique IDs, but the schema and exported function do not enforce that invariant.
findings.suggestion: Compare two `Set`s including their sizes, or enforce uniqueness in `AlertsSchema` and document that precondition explicitly.

findings.issue: The lifecycle commentary says `emitMetrics` remains null until the derived cell's connect effect subscribes. In the pinned reactor implementation, `scan(...)` subscribes to the source synchronously during construction, before `derived.cell` connects, so this explanation of the guard and pre-connect behavior is incorrect.
findings.suggestion: Update both this comment and the corresponding tick comment around line 317 to explain that `scan` installs the emitter synchronously and that it becomes null only after scan/source teardown.
